findings.description: u0001 defines a distinct, non-trivial rule (headline/heading title-case capitalization with detailed part-of-speech guidance and example) that has no corresponding rule in the candidate set. It is silently dropped from clustering, leaving a governance gap for the most common headline style.

payload.example: LISRAYA Targets the Root Cause of Dermatomyositis
payload.statement: Headlines/headings use initial caps (title case): capitalize nouns, pronouns, adjectives, verbs, adverbs; lowercase articles, conjunctions, prepositions.

findings.description: u0003 (chart/infographic headlines: ALL CAPS, specific typefaces per channel) is a separate rule scope (chart/infographic only, not general headlines or the named campaign) but is not represented by any candidate rule. It risks being conflated with rule_lisraya_campaign_headline_all_caps or lost entirely.

payload.statement: Chart/infographic headlines are set in ALL CAPS, using Agenda (print) or Nunito Sans Bold+ (email fallback).

findings.description: u0005's phrase 'other than the cases above' establishes an explicit exception relation to the campaign-headline ALL CAPS rule and the chart/infographic ALL CAPS rule. This relation is not captured; the general prohibition rule should reference/except these two rules to avoid appearing self-contradictory.

Rule lacks a scope facet restricting it to the single named campaign headline 'YOU DESERVE MORE' and omits the cross-reference to section 1.6 present in the source, making the rule read as broader than intended.

Body copy sentence-case is stated plainly in u0004 but is reinforced as an absolute prohibition (must_not use ALL CAPS for body copy) in u0005 [GENERAL]. Given the paired hard_constraint/must_not rule enforces this without exception, classifying the positive rule as only 'strong_default' understates its actual hardness; should likely be hard_constraint/must to match the paired negative rule.
